questions.question: Which personality disorders fall under DSM-5-TR Cluster A?
options: A. Paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal
B. Antisocial, borderline, histrionic, narcissistic
C. Avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive
D. Bipolar and major depressive
questions.rationale: Cluster A ('odd/eccentric') includes paranoid, schizoid, and schizotypal personality disorders.

questions.question: Which personality disorders fall under DSM-5-TR Cluster B?
options: A. Paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal
B. Antisocial, borderline, histrionic, narcissistic
C. Avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive
D. Panic and PTSD
questions.rationale: Cluster B ('dramatic/emotional/erratic') includes antisocial, borderline, histrionic, and narcissistic personality disorders.

questions.question: Which personality disorders fall under DSM-5-TR Cluster C?
options: A. Paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal
B. Antisocial, borderline, histrionic, narcissistic
C. Avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive
D. Schizophrenia spectrum
questions.rationale: Cluster C ('anxious/fearful') includes avoidant, dependent, and obsessive-compulsive personality disorders.

questions.question: Are field providers expected to diagnose a patient's specific personality disorder?
options: A. Yes, this is a required field skill
B. No — this is recognition-level exam vocabulary; field care focuses on calm, consistent, non-judgmental care
C. Only paramedics diagnose personality disorders
D. Personality disorder diagnosis is irrelevant to any EMS context
questions.rationale: This framework is recognition-level material; providers are not expected to diagnose, only to maintain consistent calm care.

questions.question: When does delirium tremens (DTs) classically begin relative to the last drink?
options: A. Within the first few hours
B. 48-96 hours after the last drink
C. Only after 2 weeks
D. DTs occurs only during active drinking
questions.rationale: DTs classically begins 48-96 hours after the last drink, later than milder withdrawal symptoms which can start within hours.

questions.question: What are the features of delirium tremens?
options: A. Severe confusion, autonomic instability, tremor, visual/tactile hallucinations
B. Mild anxiety and tremor only
C. Isolated hypertension without other findings
D. Painless, asymptomatic confusion
questions.rationale: DTs features severe confusion/disorientation, autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, fever, diaphoresis), tremor, and hallucinations.

questions.question: Is delirium tremens a life-threatening condition?
options: A. No, it always resolves without treatment
B. Yes — it carries meaningful mortality risk if untreated
C. Only in patients under 30
D. Only if hallucinations are visual, not tactile
questions.rationale: DTs is a true medical emergency with meaningful mortality risk, requiring prompt recognition and treatment.

questions.question: What is the classic presentation of stimulant intoxication?
options: A. Pinpoint pupils and respiratory depression
B. Agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, dilated pupils
C. Slow heart rate and constricted pupils
D. Isolated nausea without other findings
questions.rationale: Stimulant intoxication classically presents with agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, and dilated pupils.

questions.question: What is the classic presentation of opioid intoxication?
options: A. Agitation and dilated pupils
B. Respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, altered mental status
C. Hypertension and tachycardia
D. Hyperthermia and diaphoresis
questions.rationale: Opioid intoxication classically presents with respiratory depression, pinpoint (miotic) pupils, and altered mental status.

questions.question: Why should vital signs and glucose still be checked in a patient with an obvious substance-related presentation?
options: A. It is unnecessary once a substance cause is presumed
B. Substance-related presentations can coexist with trauma, psychiatric illness, or an unrelated medical emergency
C. Vital signs are only relevant for cardiac patients
D. Glucose is irrelevant to substance-related presentations
questions.rationale: A presumed substance cause does not exclude a coexisting or alternative medical problem, so standard assessment continues.

questions.question: What cardiac risk is associated with eating disorders?
options: A. Arrhythmia from electrolyte disturbances (e.g., hypokalemia from purging or malnutrition)
B. No cardiac risk is associated with eating disorders
C. Only hypertension, never arrhythmia
D. Risk only occurs in male patients
questions.rationale: Electrolyte disturbances (like hypokalemia) from purging behaviors or malnutrition create real cardiac arrhythmia risk in eating disorder patients.

questions.question: A patient with a known eating disorder reports palpitations and dizziness. How should this be evaluated?
options: A. Dismissed as anxiety related to their eating disorder
B. Evaluated with the same seriousness as any other patient, including ECG/monitoring where available, given real arrhythmia risk
C. Ignored unless the patient loses consciousness
D. Treated only with reassurance and no assessment
questions.rationale: Cardiac symptoms in eating disorder patients reflect a genuine medical risk and should be evaluated thoroughly, not dismissed as purely psychiatric.

questions.question: Why do high-risk substance-related presentations require heightened vigilance beyond standard de-escalation?
options: A. Intoxicated or withdrawing patients can deteriorate quickly and unpredictably
B. These patients are always calm and predictable
C. Vigilance is unnecessary once de-escalation begins
D. Only trauma patients require vigilance
questions.rationale: Because intoxicated/withdrawing patients can change status rapidly, heightened vigilance is added to standard de-escalation practices.

questions.question: A patient is 60 hours out from their last alcoholic drink and now shows severe confusion, tremor, tachycardia, hypertension, and visual hallucinations. What should this be recognized as?
options: A. Mild, expected alcohol withdrawal requiring only reassurance
B. Delirium tremens — a life-threatening emergency requiring prompt recognition and supportive care
C. A primary psychotic disorder unrelated to alcohol use
D. Normal anxiety with no medical significance
questions.rationale: The 48-96 hour timing plus severe confusion, autonomic instability, and hallucinations are classic for DTs, a life-threatening emergency distinct from mild withdrawal.

questions.question: What common approach reduces escalation risk across personality-disorder, substance-related, and DTs presentations?
options: A. Consistent, calm, non-judgmental care regardless of the underlying condition
B. Confrontational questioning to establish authority
C. Avoiding all interaction with the patient
D. Immediate physical restraint in every case
questions.rationale: Calm, consistent, non-judgmental care is the common thread that reduces escalation risk across these varied high-risk presentations.
